title: SEGMENT 3 — The players: NHGRI, Sanger, Genoscope, RIKEN, BGI, and the consortium structure

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The players: NHGRI, Sanger, Genoscope, RIKEN, BGI, and the consortium structure
Let me walk you around the world and introduce you to the labs that actually did the work, because the Human Genome Project was not one lab or one country.
It was a network, and the network mattered.
Start in Bethesda, Maryland.
The National Human Genome Research Institute, NHGRI, is one of the twenty-seven institutes of the National Institutes of Health.
NHGRI did not do most of the sequencing itself; it funded and coordinated.
The big U.

S. sequencing centers — the ones that actually generated the bases — were the Whitehead Institute Center for Genome Research at MIT under Eric Lander, the Washington University Genome Sequencing Center in St.
Louis under Robert Waterston, and Baylor College of Medicine in Houston under Richard Gibbs.
These three U.
S. centers together produced about half of the public consortium's data.
Cross the Atlantic.

The Wellcome Trust Sanger Centre, founded in 1992 on a converted manor estate in Hinxton, just outside Cambridge, England, was directed by John Sulston — who had previously sequenced the genome of the nematode worm C. elegans.
The Sanger Centre under Sulston was the single largest contributor to the public draft, producing about a third of all the sequence.

Sulston was a fierce defender of the open-data principle — we will come back to him in the Bermuda segment — and the culture of the Sanger Centre, with its insistence on public deposit of every read within twenty-four hours, set the tone for the entire consortium.
Cross the Channel.
Genoscope, the French National Sequencing Center, was established in 1996 in Evry, south of Paris, under Jean Weissenbach.
The French contribution was smaller in volume than the U.
S. or U.
K. centers, but Genoscope took responsibility for chromosome 14 in its entirety and contributed to several others.
Germany contributed through a consortium led by the Max Planck Institute and the German Cancer Research Center, focusing on chromosome 21 and parts of others.
The German chromosome 21 group, led by Marie-Laure Yaspo and André Rosenthal, finished the second-ever complete human chromosome sequence in 2000.
Japan's RIKEN Genomic Sciences Center under Yoshiyuki Sakaki took on parts of chromosomes 21 and 22 and several other regions.

And in 1999, near the end of the draft phase, China joined the consortium through the Beijing Genomics Institute, BGI, which contributed about one percent of the sequence — a small fraction, but a critical political and scientific entry that established BGI as a player, and BGI would go on to become one of the largest sequencing operations on earth.
Here is the analogy I want you to hold for the structure.

The Human Genome Project was organized like a continental railroad — different national companies, working on assigned segments, but all needing to lay down the same gauge of track so that the final assembly would meet up.

The chromosomes were divided as assignments — Sanger took chromosomes 1, 6, 9, 10, 13, 20, 22, and X; Washington University took 7, 16, Y; Whitehead took 17; Baylor took 3, 12; Genoscope took 14; the Japanese took parts of 21 and 22; and so on.

Each center mapped, sequenced, and finished their assigned chromosomes, and the data flowed into a central deposit at GenBank, the public sequence database run by the National Center for Biotechnology Information at NIH.
The coordination meetings — there were dozens of them over the years — were where the gauge of the track got agreed upon.
What clone library?
What sequencing protocol?
What quality threshold counts as finished?
What goes into GenBank when?
And then, in May 1998, into the middle of this carefully coordinated public effort, walked Craig Venter, and the entire project nearly came apart.
